title: Okroshka
subtitle: Russian cold summer soup
excerpt: Diced cucumber, egg, potato, spring onion, and smoked sausage in a cold, tangy yoghurt broth. Russia's summer soup, sharp, cooling, and made in minutes once everything is prepped and chilled.
sourceNotes: Okroshka is traditionally made with kvas, a lightly fermented drink made from bread, with a mild tang and a small amount of alcohol. It is the most popular summer soup in Russia, eaten cold as a refreshing main course on hot days. The kvas base gives authentic okroshka its characteristic sourness and slight fizz. This version replaces the kvas with plain yoghurt thinned with cold water and a small amount of vinegar, which produces a credible result without requiring home-fermented kvas.

content.text: Okroshka is not a cooked soup: it is cold components assembled in a bowl and covered with a cold, slightly sour liquid. The quality depends almost entirely on the freshness of the vegetables and the balance of the liquid. It should be tangy and cooling, not too thick, with enough acid to taste refreshing. The ingredients are diced uniformly small so every spoonful contains all the components.
The yoghurt liquid must be thoroughly cold before serving. Mix it ahead and refrigerate for at least an hour. All the components must also be cold, nothing warm goes into okroshka.

Whisk the {{plain-yoghurt}} with 500 ml of cold water and the {{cider-vinegar}} until smooth. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}}. Taste, the liquid should be pleasantly sour and lightly creamy. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour.

Ensure all components are thoroughly cold. Divide the diced {{cucumber}}, {{eggs}}, {{potato}}, {{smoked-pork-sausage}}, {{spring-onion}}, and {{dill}} between four chilled bowls. Season with {{black-pepper-ground}}. Pour the cold yoghurt liquid over each bowl at the table. Serve immediately with bread.

items.fix: Add more cider vinegar a teaspoon at a time and taste. The liquid should have a clear, pleasant sourness, more than yoghurt usually provides on its own
items.cause: Too much yoghurt relative to water and vinegar
items.symptom: Soup tastes flat and milky rather than tangy

items.fix: Okroshka is a fairly liquid soup, the components sit at the bottom and the liquid is poured over. Aim for about 200 ml of liquid per bowl, which allows the components to float slightly
Do not add the liquid to the components in advance. Pour at the table, just before eating
items.cause: Cucumber releasing juice into the base
items.symptom: Cucumber making the liquid watery after sitting

content.text: Okroshka is the soup Russian families eat in summer when the heat makes anything warm unappealing. It is made in the morning, refrigerated, and poured over the cold components at lunchtime. The authentic version uses kvas, a lightly fermented bread drink with a tangy, almost earthy quality, and when made with real kvas the soup has a complexity that the yoghurt version approximates but does not fully replicate. Both are worth eating. Okroshka is best understood as a cold main course rather than a soup in the usual sense: it is a meal poured from a jug.
